core_intuitions: RL-for-LLMs rewards are sparse and terminal: a single scalar arrives at EOS and every intermediate reward is zero, so the entire learning signal is one number per rollout that must be turned into a per-token advantage.

The three production estimator families (Monte-Carlo/RLOO, GAE, GRPO) differ ONLY in how they convert that one terminal number into a per-token A_t: a scalar baseline, a learned per-position critic, or a within-group mean/std.
Every reduction (whitening, KL, entropy, loss) must be computed over the MASKED non-pad tokens; averaging over padding silently rescales the statistics by batch composition.

The k1/k2/k3 KL estimators trade off unbiased-but-can-go-negative (k1) vs biased-but-nonnegative (k2) vs unbiased-AND-nonnegative (k3, via a zero-mean control variate) — which is why k3 is the modern default.

PPO clipping is asymmetric by construction (the min): it only ever removes incentive to move further in a direction already moved a lot, and never flips the gradient's sign; clip-higher and dual-clip refine the two ends.

Loss aggregation (token-mean vs sequence-mean) silently changes the objective: token-mean lets long sequences dominate the gradient and drives length inflation, sequence-mean weights each response equally.

new_figures.name: advkl-kl-estimators-k1k2k3
new_figures.concept: The three KL estimators k1, k2, k3 plotted as functions of the per-token log-ratio, showing which go negative, which are biased, and which is both unbiased and non-negative.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter's headline KL recommendation is 'use k3 by default: unbiased AND non-negative'. The words 'unbiased but can be negative' (k1), 'biased but always positive' (k2), and 'unbiased and always positive' (k3) only become intuitive when you SEE the three curves against the x-axis: k1 crossing below zero, k2 as a symmetric bowl that overshoots, and k3 as the asymmetric convex curve that hugs zero yet never dips under it. A single static plot makes the entire k1/k2/k3 discussion click and justifies the default choice at a glance.
new_figures.placement: Section 'The k1 / k2 / k3 estimators', immediately after the kl_estimators code block / before the 'Why is k3 unbiased' aside.
new_figures.brief: A single 2D line plot. Horizontal axis: the per-token log-ratio r = log(pi_ref / pi_theta), centered at 0, ranging roughly r in [-1.2, 1.2] with a zero gridline drawn prominently; label the axis 'r = log(pi_ref / pi_theta)  (0 = policy matches reference)'. Vertical axis: 'per-token KL estimate', with a horizontal zero line. Plot THREE curves, each visually distinct WITHOUT relying on hue alone (use solid / dashed / dotted line styles plus theme-safe accent tokens, and label each curve directly with a small tag at its right end): (1) k1 = -r, a straight line with negative slope passing through the origin; SHADE the region where k1 dips below the zero line (r>0) and annotate it 'k1 goes NEGATIVE here -- a KL penalty that pays the policy to move away'. (2) k2 = 0.5 * r^2, a symmetric upward bowl touching zero only at r=0, always >= 0; tag it 'k2: always >= 0 but BIASED (overshoots true KL)'. (3) k3 = e^r - 1 - r, a convex asymmetric curve that also touches zero only at r=0, stays >= 0 everywhere, rises steeply for r>0 and gently for r<0; tag it 'k3: unbiased AND >= 0 (the default)'. Draw a light vertical band around r ~ 0 (e.g. |r| < 0.3) labeled 'KL-control regime: distributions close -- k2 ~ k3 ~ true KL', where the three curves nearly coincide, visually showing why k2 is a fine local approximation. Small caption underneath: 'k3 = k1 + (e^r - 1): the e^r-1 term is a zero-mean control variate -- same expectation as k1, but it cancels the variance and floors the estimate at zero.' Use only mathematical values on the axes (small ticks like -1, 0, 1); invent no benchmark numbers. Everything must fit the viewBox with no curve or label clipping at the plot edges, in both light and dark themes.

new_figures.name: advkl-loss-aggregation-length
new_figures.concept: How token-mean vs sequence-mean aggregation distributes gradient weight across a short and a long correct response for the same prompt, and why token-mean drives length inflation.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter calls loss aggregation 'the most underappreciated and most consequential implementation choice in the chapter' and stresses it 'silently changes what objective you are optimizing.' The worked example (10-token vs 200-token, 95% vs 50% of the gradient) is exactly the kind of thing that is far more visceral as a picture than as prose. A side-by-side of the same two sequences under the two denominators makes the length-bias failure mode -- the spine of the GRPO length-explosion story -- immediately legible.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Loss aggregation: token-mean vs sequence-mean', after the two aggregation formulas / near the aggregate_loss code block.
new_figures.brief: A two-panel comparison sharing the same input at top. INPUT strip (top, shared): two token strips for the same prompt, both marked 'correct (A > 0)': a SHORT completion drawn as a few boxes (e.g. 3 small boxes, label 'short: ~10 tokens') and a LONG completion drawn as many boxes (e.g. 9 boxes, label 'long: ~200 tokens'), each box a per-token surrogate loss pushing the token up. Keep the token counts small and clearly illustrative; annotate 'per-token loss identical in both'. LEFT panel titled 'Token-mean (sum over tokens / total tokens)': render a horizontal gradient-contribution bar split proportionally so the long sequence occupies the large majority of the bar (roughly 3 : 9 -> long ~75%); label the two segments 'short' and 'long (dominates)'. Below it a bold takeaway with an up-arrow on length: 'long correct answers accrue more total gradient -> the model learns longer = better -> length creeps up.' RIGHT panel titled 'Sequence-mean (mean per sequence, then mean over sequences)': render the gradient-contribution bar split exactly 50 / 50 between short and long; label 'each response counts once'. Below: 'every response equal regardless of length -> per-token gradient in the long chain is DOWN-weighted (can under-train long reasoning).' Bottom caption spanning both: 'Same data, same per-token losses -- different gradient, different length dynamics. Find the loss-reduction line first when reading a codebase.' Use theme-safe fills, no hardcoded primaries; the two bars must be visually comparable (same total width). Keep all labels legible and inside the viewBox in both themes; ensure the proportional split reads clearly at small sizes.

new_figures.name: advkl-three-advantage-families
new_figures.concept: The same terminal-reward rollout group turned into a per-token advantage three different ways: Monte-Carlo/RLOO (flat scalar baseline), GAE (per-token critic), and GRPO (within-group standardization).
new_figures.rationale: The entire section 'Monte-Carlo, GAE, and Group-Relative Advantages' is organized around one idea from the chapter: the three families 'differ entirely in HOW they turn that one terminal number into a per-token A_t.' The prior figure shows a single rollout's terminal reward broadcasting to tokens; this figure shows the three DISTINCT machineries side by side -- critic vs no-critic, per-token vs constant-per-sequence, single-sample vs group -- which is the mental model a reader needs before reading any advantage-computation function. It ties directly to the cheat-sheet table and to why GRPO displaced the critic for verifiable-reward reasoning.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Monte-Carlo, GAE, and Group-Relative Advantages', at the top of the section (before the Monte-Carlo subsection).
new_figures.brief: Three columns, each starting from the SAME terminal reward and ending in a per-token advantage profile drawn as a small row of token cells whose height/fill encodes A_t. COLUMN 1 'Monte-Carlo / RLOO -- scalar baseline, no critic': show one rollout with terminal reward R, a baseline b (small tag: 'batch mean, running mean, or RLOO leave-one-out mean of the other K-1 samples'), and an output row where EVERY token cell has the SAME height = R - b (flat). Badge: 'no critic; constant per sequence; unbiased.' COLUMN 2 'GAE -- learned critic V(s_t)': show the same rollout plus a small per-position curve labeled 'critic V(s_t)'; the output row has cells of VARYING height = A_t^GAE, drawn as a smooth per-token profile (illustratively different per position). Small note: 'gamma=1, lambda=1 for terminal reward -> every token gets R - V(s_t); the critic is a position-dependent baseline.' Badge: 'critic needed; per-token resolution; bias/variance knob via lambda.' COLUMN 3 'GRPO -- group-relative, critic-free': show a GROUP of several completions for the same prompt (e.g. 4 stacked mini-rollouts) each with its own reward R_1..R_G; show subtracting the group mean and dividing by group std -> each completion gets a single standardized scalar A_i, and its token row is flat at that value (different flat level per completion). Small note: 'group mean = baseline, group std = built-in per-prompt normalization; all-same-reward group -> A=0 -> no gradient.' Badge: 'no critic; constant per sequence (outcome); std-norm bias.' Keep the three token-advantage rows using a shared, theme-safe diverging encoding (above-baseline vs below-baseline) with a tiny legend 'push up / push down'. Use only small illustrative integers for token counts and group size; invent no benchmark numbers. Ensure the three columns align on a common baseline, read left-to-right, and fit the viewBox without overlap in both light and dark themes.

summary: This chapter is concept-rich but figure-poor (2 existing figures for a dense engineering reference). Both existing figures render correctly and clearly in light and dark -- advkl-terminal-reward-rollout nails the sparse/terminal-reward intuition and advkl-ppo-clip-quadrant cleanly conveys PPO's asymmetric four-case min -- so both are KEEP with no changes. The gaps are three foundational intuitions with no visual support: (1) the k1/k2/k3 KL estimator tradeoff, which becomes obvious as a single function plot showing k1 dipping negative, k2 as a biased bowl, and k3 as the unbiased non-negative curve -- justifying the k3 default; (2) the token-mean vs sequence-mean loss-aggregation choice, explicitly flagged as the most consequential implementation decision and the driver of length inflation, best shown as a short-vs-long gradient-split comparison; and (3) the three advantage-estimator families as three distinct machineries turning one terminal reward into per-token A_t, the organizing spine of the estimator section. All three are static -- no motion is essential here. Proposed: 2 priority-1 static figures and 1 priority-2 static figure; no animations, no replacements.
